title: Perfect spin nonreciprocity in gated superconducting altermagnetic heterostructures
summary: We consider a superconducting altermagnet heterostructure and demonstrate that the interplay between altermagnetism and a selective filter of transverse momentum channels enables perfect nonreciprocal spin-polarized currents. We demonstrate that this nonreciprocity manifests in both local and nonlocal spin currents, signalling the emergence of directionally selective local and nonlocal spin behaviors. We show that the selective filter of transverse momentum channels is realized by gating a finite normal region between the superconducting altermagnet and the metallic reservoir, which then directionally selects transport channels that match the momentum-dependent spin-split superconducting altermagnetic states, allowing for nonreciprocal spin-polarized currents. We discover that the local and nonlocal spin nonreciprocity features a highly tunable polarity and nearly perfect quality factors, respectively, which is achieved by means of gate voltages and by varying the length of the finite region. Moreover, we find that local and nonlocal charge currents also develop a nonreciprocal behavior, whose quality factors can also reach perfect values. In all cases, the spin and charge currents are sensitive to variations of the altermagnetic field, a functional dependence that can be exploited to identify the type of altermagnetism. Our findings put forward an electrically controllable route towards nonreciprocal superconducting spintronic devices based on altermagnets.

AI.tldr: A study on superconducting altermagnet heterostructures showing that combining altermagnetism with transverse momentum filtering enables perfect nonreciprocal spin-polarized currents. The effect is tunable via gate voltages and altermagnetic field, offering a pathway for nonreciprocal superconducting spintronic devices.
AI.motivation: The field aims to achieve perfect nonreciprocal spin-polarized currents, which are fundamental for advanced spintronic devices that can control electron spin and charge transport with high precision.
AI.method: The researchers combined altermagnetism with a selective momentum filter in a superconducting heterostructure, using gate voltages and varying the length of a finite normal region between the altermagnet and metallic reservoir to tune transport properties.
AI.result: Demonstrated perfect nonreciprocity in both local and nonlocal spin and charge currents with nearly perfect quality factors, achieved through gate voltage control and altermagnetic field variation.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes an electrically controllable pathway for nonreciprocal superconducting spintronic devices using altermagnets, with potential applications in spin-based electronics and quantum transport.

title: Discovery of parity-violating chiral polar-nematic charge density wave and superconductivity in kagome metals
summary: Nonmagnetic kagome metals and superconductors AV3Sb5 (A = K, Rb, Cs) host unconventional charge density wave (CDW) and superconducting (SC) phases accompanied by multiple electronic symmetry breaking. Due to the centrosymmetric crystal structure, inversion symmetry has generally been assumed to hold. Here, using scanning tunneling microscopy complemented by atomic force microscopy and optical second-harmonic generation, we directly reveal that inversion symmetry in the kagome plane is spontaneously broken in the CDW state. The mixed-parity CDW state exhibits ferroelectric dipolar and nematic quadrupolar ordered moments. The coexistence and coupling between the dipole and quadrupole favor noncollinear ferro-polar and nematic alignment that breaks all mirror symmetries and gives rise to robust electronic chirality in the 3Q CDW. The multipolar coupling to in-plane electric field enables electric field control and manipulation of the chiral polar-nematic CDW state, including its chirality. Below the SC transition, we observe parity-violating pair density modulations at both the original and the CDW lattice wavevectors. Our findings of parity-violating electronic chiral multipolar order provide microscopic insights into the magnetoelectric and nonreciprocal transport, loop current order, pairing density waves, and unconventional superconductivity in kagome metals and related quantum materials.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers that inversion symmetry is spontaneously broken in the charge density wave (CDW) state of kagome metals AV3Sb5 (A = K, Rb, Cs), revealing a mixed-parity CDW state with ferroelectric dipolar and nematic quadrupolar ordered moments that gives rise to electronic chirality. The findings provide microscopic insights into unconventional superconductivity and related phenomena in these materials.
AI.motivation: The researchers challenged the assumption that centrosymmetric crystal structures maintain inversion symmetry. They sought to directly probe whether inversion symmetry is broken in the CDW state of kagome metals, as this has implications for understanding unconventional phases like superconductivity.
AI.method: The team employed scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and optical second-harmonic generation (SHG) to directly investigate the electronic symmetry in the kagome plane of AV3Sb5 compounds.
AI.result: They discovered that inversion symmetry in the kagome plane is spontaneously broken in the CDW state. The mixed-parity CDW exhibits ferroelectric dipolar and nematic quadrupolar ordered moments. The coupling between dipole and quadrupole favors noncollinear ferro-polar and nematic alignment that breaks all mirror symmetries, giving rise to robust electronic chirality in the 3Q CDW. Below the superconducting transition, parity-violating pair density modulations were observed at both original and CDW lattice wavevectors.
AI.conclusion: These findings of parity-violating electronic chiral multipolar order provide microscopic insights into magnetoelectric and nonreciprocal transport, loop current order, pairing density waves, and unconventional superconductivity in kagome metals and related quantum materials. The multipolar coupling to in-plane electric field enables electric field control and manipulation of the chiral polar-nematic CDW state, including its chirality.

title: Superconductivity in bilayer La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$: A review focusing on the strong-coupling Hund's rule assisted pairing mechanism
summary: Discovery of high-$T_c$ superconductivity (SC) in the bilayer nickelate series La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ have attracted substantial interest, providing a new platform for exploring unconventional SC. Certain experimental evidence has pointed to a correlated electronic nature, which is the driving force responsible for its high critical temperature ($T_c$). This work reviews the SC in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$, with a particular focus on theoretical understanding of its pairing mechanism driven by this strong-coupling, Hund-assisted scenario. The electronic landscape is governed by two $E_g$-orbitals within the bilayer structure of NiO$_2$ planes. The $3d_{z^2}$ orbital is nearly half-filled and exhibits a stronger localized character, while the $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ is approximately quarter-filled and remains highly itinerant. The localized $3d_{z^2}$ orbitals experience robust interlayer hybridization, mediated by the $2p_z$ orbitals of the inner apical oxygen atoms. This hybridization generates a strong interlayer antiferromagnetic (AFM) exchange. In the strong coupling regime, Hund's rule coupling aligns the spins of the two $E_g$ orbitals on the same nickel site. The strong interlayer AFM exchange is effectively transferred to the itinerant $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbital, generating an effective coupling $J_{\perp}$ within this orbital. This mechanism is captured by a minimal strong-coupling bilayer $t$-$J$-$J_{\perp}$ model for the $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ band. Driven by $J_{\perp}$, $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ electrons can form interlayer Cooper pairs, leading to an extended $s$-wave pairing SC with high $T_c$. Meanwhile, the strongly localized $3d_{z^2}$ electrons tend to form interlayer rung singlets. Due to a lack of phase coherence, these singlets do not directly participate in the SC condensate, but instead give rise to a pseudogap phase.

AI.tldr: This review paper proposes a strong-coupling, Hund-assisted pairing mechanism for high-Tc superconductivity in bilayer nickelate La₃Ni₂O₇. The interlayer antiferromagnetic exchange between localized 3dz² orbitals transfers to the itinerant 3dx²-y² band via Hund's coupling, driving interlayer Cooper pair formation and extended s-wave superconductivity.
AI.motivation: The discovery of high-Tc superconductivity in La₃Ni₂O₇ provides a new platform for exploring unconventional superconductivity. Experimental evidence points to correlated electronic nature as the driving force for high Tc, but the pairing mechanism needed theoretical clarification.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using a minimal strong-coupling bilayer t-J-J⊥ model for the 3dx²-y² band, combined with examination of electronic structure, orbital hybridization, and Hund's rule coupling
AI.result: Extended s-wave pairing superconductivity with high Tc driven by interlayer Cooper pairs in the 3dx²-y² band, with a pseudogap phase arising from localized 3dz² rung singlets
AI.conclusion: The strong-coupling, Hund-assisted mechanism provides a coherent framework for understanding the high-Tc superconductivity in La₃Ni₂O₇. The interlayer AFM exchange mediated by apical oxygen 2pz orbitals transfers to the itinerant 3dx²-y² band via Hund's coupling, driving interlayer Cooper pair formation. The localized 3dz² electrons form rung singlets that create a pseudogap phase but do not contribute directly to superconductivity.

title: Stabilization of a non-superconducting, orthorhombic phase by over-hydrogenating LaFeSiH
summary: Chemical composition provides a powerful route to tune the electronic ground state of iron-based superconductors and other quantum materials, yet access to highly doped phases remains limited. Here we demonstrate that high-pressure thermal decomposition of hydrogen-rich precursors enables over-hydrogenation of LaFeSi. Using anthracene, we synthesize tetragonal superconducting LaFeSiH, including a single hydrogen site, while ammonia borane yields a structurally distorted over-hydrogenated phase, LaFeSiH1+x, with an orthorhombic structure. Chemical analysis reveal excess hydrogen (x ~ 0.6), implying a second H site in LaFeSiH1.6 whose localization and occupancy are determined by neutron diffraction. In contrast to metallic LaFeSi and superconducting LaFeSiH, orthorhombic LaFeSiH1.6 exhibits semiconductor-like behavior. Upon hydrogen release near 100 °C, it transforms into tetragonal superconducting LaFeSiH1+δ (δ << 0.6). These results establish the chemical flexibility of the layered LaFeSiX (X = H, O, F) family and provide access to a high hydrogen-doping regime, creating new opportunities to investigate superconductivity in Fe-based silicides.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that high-pressure thermal decomposition of hydrogen-rich precursors enables over-hydrogenation of LaFeSi, synthesizing superconducting LaFeSiH and orthorhombic LaFeSiH1.6 with excess hydrogen, revealing new chemical flexibility in the LaFeSiX family.
AI.motivation: Chemical composition tuning of iron-based superconductors is limited for highly doped phases. The researchers sought to access high hydrogen-doping regimes in LaFeSi to explore new superconducting phases.
AI.method: High-pressure thermal decomposition of hydrogen-rich precursors (anthracene and ammonia borane) was used to synthesize hydrogenated LaFeSi compounds. Structural characterization was done via neutron diffraction, and electrical properties were measured.
AI.result: Two phases were synthesized: tetragonal superconducting LaFeSiH (using anthracene) and orthorhombic over-hydrogenated LaFeSiH1.6 (using ammonia borane) with x ~ 0.6 excess hydrogen. LaFeSiH1.6 exhibits semiconductor-like behavior, transforming to superconducting LaFeSiH1+δ upon hydrogen release near 100°C.
AI.conclusion: The layered LaFeSiX (X = H, O, F) family has significant chemical flexibility. High-pressure hydrogenation enables access to high hydrogen-doping regimes, creating new opportunities for investigating superconductivity in Fe-based silicides.

title: Melting temperature shifts from quantum fluctuations in generalized Wigner crystals
summary: It is generally believed that quantum fluctuations collaborate with thermal fluctuations, effectively reducing transition temperatures (e.g. for melting of charge order). We show that this is not always the case and that the interplay between quantum and thermal fluctuations can be competitive. We find excellent motivation for addressing this thanks to the discovery of correlated insulating "generalized Wigner crystal" (GWC) states in hetero-bilayer transition metal dichalcogenide (WS$_2$/WSe$_2$) moiré systems [Y. Xu, et al., Nature 587, 214-218 (2020)]. We account for the impact of quantum effects on the melting temperature of GWCs, carrying out finite temperature Lanczos calculations on an extended Hubbard model on the triangular lattice (both with a double-gate screened potential, and the nearest neighbor model) for multiple electron densities. We show that quantum effects capture the shift relative to the classical estimates, which in some cases are more than 50 percent off from the experimental values. Then building on these numerical findings, we provide a qualitative picture that clarifies that while quantum melting of GWC (by increasing the bandwidth) naturally softens the ground state order parameter, it does not always decrease the melting temperature; conversely it can increase it. To do so we employ a finite temperature perturbation theory, treating the kinetic energy perturbatively on top of a classical Wigner crystal. Our predictions should be observable in future experiments where the bandwidth can be tuned.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the belief that quantum fluctuations always reduce transition temperatures, demonstrating instead that quantum and thermal fluctuations have a competitive (not collaborative) interplay. Using finite temperature Lanczos calculations on an extended Hubbard model for generalized Wigner crystals in WS₂/WSe₂ moiré systems, the authors show quantum effects can both increase or decrease melting temperatures, with classical estimates being over 50% off from experimental values in some cases.
AI.motivation: The general belief that quantum fluctuations collaborate with thermal fluctuations to reduce transition temperatures (e.g., for melting of charge order) motivated the authors to investigate whether this is always true. The discovery of correlated insulating "generalized Wigner crystal" (GWC) states in hetero-bilayer transition metal dichalcogenide moiré systems (WS₂/WSe₂) provided excellent experimental motivation.
AI.method: The authors performed finite temperature Lanczos calculations on an extended Hubbard model on the triangular lattice for multiple electron densities. They considered both double-gate screened potential and nearest neighbor models. Additionally, they employed finite temperature perturbation theory treating kinetic energy perturbatively on top of a classical Wigner crystal to provide a qualitative picture.
AI.result: Quantum effects capture the shift relative to classical estimates, which in some cases are more than 50 percent off from experimental values. While quantum melting by increasing bandwidth naturally softens the ground state order parameter, it does not always decrease the melting temperature - it can actually increase it in some cases.
AI.conclusion: The interplay between quantum and thermal fluctuations is competitive rather than collaborative. Quantum effects do not always reduce transition temperatures; they can increase melting temperatures. The qualitative picture shows that quantum softening of the ground state order parameter doesn't necessarily lead to lower melting temperatures. These predictions should be observable in future experiments where bandwidth can be tuned.

title: Organosilane-functionalized hydrothermal-derived coatings on titanium alloys for hydrophobization and corrosion protection
summary: This work focuses on the structure, wettability and corrosion behaviors of Ti-6Al-4V alloy after roughening treatments in different concentrations of NaOH aqueous solutions followed by low surface energy hexadecyltrimethoxysilane (HDTMS) coating. In this regard, scanning electron microscopy, contact angle measurements, potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were used to characterize the samples. In contrast to hydrophilicity caused by the hydrothermal alkaline treatments, the subsequent HDTMS coating donated considerable hydrophobicity. Typically, the highest sessile water contact angle (about 147 deg) was obtained for the sample treated in 3 molar NaOH solution followed by the HDTMS coating. In addition, the alkaline treatment reduced the corrosion resistance of the surface in a NaCl aqueous solution; however, the HDTMS hydrophobization process improved it significantly. It is eventually concluded that the coupled use of alkaline treatment and HDTMS functionalization can be further considered for moisture-exposed applications of Ti-based alloys.

AI.tldr: This study investigates surface modification of Ti-6Al-4V alloy through alkaline (NaOH) treatments followed by hexadecyltrimethoxysilane (HDTMS) coating to control wettability and corrosion resistance.
AI.motivation: Titanium alloys are widely used in moisture-exposed applications, but their surface properties (wettability and corrosion resistance) need optimization for improved performance and durability.
AI.method: Samples were treated in different NaOH concentrations, then coated with HDTMS. Characterization included SEM (morphology), contact angle measurements (wettability), potentiodynamic polarization and EIS (corrosion behavior).
AI.result: Alkaline treatment alone increased hydrophilicity, but HDTMS coating provided hydrophobicity (up to 147° contact angle at 3M NaOH). While NaOH treatment reduced corrosion resistance, HDTMS coating significantly improved it in NaCl solution.
AI.conclusion: The combined alkaline treatment + HDTMS functionalization is effective for enhancing hydrophobicity and corrosion resistance of Ti-6Al-4V alloy for moisture-exposed applications.

title: Field-Induced Selective Spin Gap Closure and Quantum Criticality in BaNd$_2$ZnS$_5$
summary: We report thermodynamic evidence for field-induced mode-selective quantum criticality in the layered rare-earth magnet BaNd2ZnS5 (BNZS). Below the Neel temperature TN = 2.9 K, spin-orbit-entangled Nd3+ moments form two symmetry-inequivalent low-energy spin-excitation modes arising from Kramers doublet physics under a magnetic field, with distinct gaps Delta_L and Delta_H. For magnetic fields applied along the [110] direction, the lower-energy gap Delta_L softens continuously and collapses at a critical field Hc ~ 2 T, while the higher-energy gap Delta_H remains gapped, leaving the system in an intermediate partially critical phase. Despite the partial nature of the criticality, thermodynamic measurements reveal a continuous quantum phase transition. The ac susceptibility shows universal scaling behavior, with chi_ac(T, H) collapsing onto a single scaling function and following chi_ac ~ T^-0.2 at criticality. A finite residual Sommerfeld coefficient gamma_0 further indicates the emergence of gapless excitations confined to a single symmetry sector near the quantum critical point. In contrast to conventional quantum criticality based on global softening of low-energy excitations, BNZS exhibits a selective breakdown of Kramers-doublet excitations due to its strong anisotropic interactions. Our results establish BNZS as a spin-orbit-coupled rare-earth magnet where quantum criticality is not global but mode-selective, with anisotropic interactions enabling independent criticality in distinct excitation sectors.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the discovery of mode-selective quantum criticality in the layered rare-earth magnet BaNd2ZnS5 (BNZS), where only one of two symmetry-inequivalent spin-excitation modes becomes critical under magnetic field, in contrast to conventional quantum criticality where all low-energy excitations soften globally.
AI.motivation: Conventional quantum criticality theory assumes global softening of all low-energy excitations at the critical point. This study investigates whether quantum criticality can occur selectively in specific excitation sectors in spin-orbit-coupled rare-earth magnets with strong anisotropic interactions.
AI.method: Thermodynamic measurements (ac susceptibility) were performed on BaNd2ZnS5 below TN = 2.9 K with magnetic field applied along the [110] direction. The field-dependent behavior of two distinct spin-excitation gaps (Delta_L and Delta_H) was probed, and scaling analysis of chi_ac(T, H) was conducted near the critical field Hc ~ 2 T.
AI.result: At Hc ~ 2 T, the lower-energy gap Delta_L softens continuously and collapses while the higher-energy gap Delta_H remains gapped, producing an intermediate partially critical phase. The ac susceptibility exhibits universal scaling with chi_ac ~ T^-0.3, and a finite residual Sommerfeld coefficient gamma_0 indicates gapless excitations confined to a single symmetry sector.
AI.conclusion: BaNd2ZnS5 represents a new class of spin-orbit-coupled rare-earth magnets exhibiting mode-selective quantum criticality, where anisotropic interactions enable independent critical behavior in distinct excitation sectors. This challenges conventional understanding of quantum phase transitions and provides a platform for exploring selective criticality in strongly correlated electron systems.

title: Griffiths-like phase, spin-phonon coupling, and exchange-bias in the disordered double perovskite GdSrCoMnO$_{6}$
summary: We report structural, magnetic, and Raman studies of the disordered double perovskite GdSrCoMnO$_{6}$~(GSCM). DC magnetization shows a ferromagnetic transition at $T_{C} \approx 153$~K. The inverse susceptibility exhibits a downturn above $T_{C}$ and is consistent with a Griffiths-like regime extending up to $T_{G} \approx 172$~K. Raman measurements show a deviation of the phonon frequency from the anharmonic background near the magnetic-ordering region, consistent with spin-phonon coupling. AC susceptibility indicates slow magnetic dynamics below the freezing temperature $T_{f} \approx 30$~K. These results point to magnetic inhomogeneity generated by the random distribution of mixed-valence Co and Mn ions and by the resulting competition between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic interactions. In the low-temperature regime, an exchange-bias effect is observed up to 50~K, with an exchange-bias magnitude $|H_{EB}| = 379$~Oe at 5~K. Structural disorder therefore plays an important role in the magnetic correlations, spin dynamics, and spin-lattice response of GSCM.

AI.tldr: The study reports structural, magnetic, and Raman investigations of the disordered double perovskite GdSrCoMnO6 (GSCM). It reveals a ferromagnetic transition at ~153 K, a Griffiths-like regime up to ~172 K, spin-phonon coupling observed via phonon frequency shifts near the magnetic ordering, slow magnetic dynamics below ~30 K, and an exchange-bias effect up to 50 K (|H_EB| = 379 Oe at 5 K). The results highlight the significance of structural disorder from mixed-valence Co/Mn ions in shaping magnetic correlations, dynamics, and spin-lattice response.
AI.motivation: The research aims to understand how structural disorder and random distribution of mixed-valence transition-metal ions influence magnetic interactions, spin dynamics, and spin-lattice coupling in double perovskite oxides, specifically to explore the nature of magnetic inhomogeneity and the competition between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic interactions.
AI.method: DC magnetization measurements were performed to determine the ferromagnetic transition temperature (Tc) and theGriffiths-like regime. AC susceptibility measurements were used to investigate slow magnetic dynamics and freezing temperature (Tf). Raman spectroscopy was employed to probe phonon behavior and spin-phonon coupling. Additionally, exchange-bias measurements were conducted in the low-temperature regime.
AI.result: A ferromagnetic transition was observed at Tc ≈ 153 K, with a Griffiths-like behavior extending up to Tg ≈ 172 K. Raman spectra showed anomalous phonon frequency shifts near the magnetic ordering region, indicating spin-phonon coupling. AC susceptibility revealed slow dynamics below the freezing temperature Tf ≈ 30 K. An exchange-bias effect was detected up to 50 K, with a magnitude of |H_EB| = 379 Oe at 5 K.
AI.conclusion: Structural disorder, arising from the random distribution of mixed-valence Co and Mn ions, plays a pivotal role in determining the magnetic correlations, spin dynamics, and spin-lattice response of GSCM, leading to complex magnetic behavior such as Griffiths-phase signatures, spin-glass-like dynamics, and exchange-bias.

title: Symmetry breaking phases and transitions in an Ising fusion category lattice model
summary: An anyon-chain-like lattice model with symmetry described by the Ising fusion category is studied. Combining numerical and analytical studies, we uncover a rich phase diagram that contains three phases: a symmetric critical phase and two categorical symmetry breaking phases. The symmetric phase lies in the same universality class as the usual critical Ising model. The first symmetry-breaking phase, dubbed the \emph{categorical ferromagnetic} phase, has the Ising fusion category fully broken and exhibits a threefold ground-state degeneracy, as expected from the generalized Landau paradigm. The other symmetry-breaking phase is analogous to a conventional antiferromagnet: it breaks lattice translation and part of the Ising fusion category, and therefore is termed the \emph{categorical antiferromagnetic} phase. Unlike ordinary antiferromagnetic states associated with finite invertible symmetry breaking, this phase itself is critical, being described by a fourfold degenerate Ising conformal field theory. We argue more generally that antiferromagnetic states associated with broken non-invertible symmetries have a large low-energy manifold that grows exponentially in system size, due to the greater-than-one quantum dimension of domain walls. We also numerically study the transitions between the three phases. The transition between the symmetric and categorical ferromagnetic phase is described by the $c=7/10$ tricritical Ising CFT, while the transition between the symmetric and categorical antiferromagnetic phases is less understood. Our numerical data suggest that the latter transition is continuous and described by a conformal field theory with central charge $c=3/2$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a lattice model with Ising fusion category symmetry, uncovering three distinct quantum phases: a symmetric critical phase, a categorical ferromagnetic phase with threefold degeneracy, and an unusual categorical antiferromagnetic phase that is critical with exponentially large low-energy manifold due to non-invertible symmetry breaking. The phase transitions are characterized by different conformal field theories.
AI.motivation: Investigate phase structure and transitions in lattice models with non-invertible (categorical) symmetries, particularly Ising fusion category, which goes beyond conventional Landau symmetry-breaking paradigm.
AI.method: Combined numerical and analytical studies of an anyon-chain lattice model with Ising fusion category symmetry.
AI.result: Three phases identified: symmetric critical phase (Ising universality), categorical ferromagnetic phase (3-fold degeneracy), and categorical antiferromagnetic phase (4-fold degenerate Ising CFT, critical). Transitions characterized: c=7/10 tricritical Ising CFT for ferromagnetic transition; c=3/2 CFT for antiferromagnetic transition.
AI.conclusion: The model reveals three distinct phases with unique characteristics: a symmetric critical phase (Ising CFT), a categorical ferromagnetic phase (threefold degeneracy, symmetry fully broken), and a categorical antiferromagnetic phase (critical with 4-fold degenerate Ising CFT). The transitions between phases are described by different CFTs: c=7/10 for symmetric to ferromagnetic, and c=3/2 (with exponential low-energy manifold) for symmetric to antiferromagnetic.

title: Accelerating the Design of Resorbable Magnesium Alloys: A Machine Learning Approach to Property Prediction
summary: Resorbable magnesium (Mg) alloys are promising candidates for temporary medical devices due to their biodegradability and favorable mechanical properties. To accelerate the design of diluted Mg alloys for implants, we developed a data-driven framework to elucidate the complex relationships between composition, processing, and mechanical properties. The framework screens mechanical properties within biocompatible compositional limits, treating degradation as a design constraint rather than an explicit prediction target. Using a dataset of 410 samples, we trained six different machine learning (ML) models to predict yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation. Among them, ensemble models, particularly CatBoost, demonstrated high predictive accuracy (R2, YS = 0.950, UTS = 0.916 and El = 0.903). SHapley Additive exPlanation analysis revealed that thermomechanical processing conditions and alloying elements such as Zn, Mn and Gd are the most influential factors governing mechanical behavior in diluted Mg alloys. Validation on the experimental dataset confirmed the models' robustness and generalization capability in capturing process-property relationships. The optimized CatBoost model was further employed to generate predictive property maps visualizing the strength-ductility trade-off as a function of Zn-Mn composition. This work establishes a validated ML framework for rapid in silico screening and targeted design of next-generation resorbable Mg alloys.

AI.tldr: A data-driven ML framework using CatBoost to predict mechanical properties (yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, elongation) of resorbable Mg alloys, identifying thermomechanical processing conditions and alloying elements (Zn, Mn, Gd) as key influencers.
AI.motivation: Resorbable magnesium alloys are promising candidates for temporary medical devices due to biodegradability and favorable mechanical properties. However, designing diluted Mg alloys with optimal properties requires understanding complex composition-processing-property relationships, which is time-consuming experimentally.
AI.method: Used a dataset of 410 samples to train six ML models (ensemble models including CatBoost) for predicting yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation. Applied SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) analysis to identify influential factors. Treated degradation as a design constraint. Generated predictive property maps for Zn-Mn composition optimization.
AI.result: CatBoost demonstrated highest predictive accuracy with R² = 0.950 (yield strength), 0.916 (ultimate tensile strength), and 0.903 (elongation). SHAP analysis revealed thermomechanical processing conditions and alloying elements Zn, Mn, and Gd as most influential factors governing mechanical behavior. Validation confirmed models' robustness and generalization capability.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a validated ML framework for rapid in silico screening and targeted design of next-generation resorbable Mg alloys, enabling visualization of strength-ductility trade-off as a function of composition.

title: Disorder-driven coexistence of distinct dynamical states in frustrated Sr$_3$CuNb$_2$O$_9$: a microscopic $μ$SR and $^{93}$Nb NMR study
summary: Despite recent progress in identifying the exotic random singlet (RS) state in disordered frustrated magnets as a distinct correlated phase, three-dimensional (3D) realizations remain scarce. Sr$_3$CuNb$_2$O$_9$ was proposed to be one of such 3D frustrated systems with magnetic site disorder hosting an RS ground state. Here, we report a detailed microscopic investigation of Sr$_3$CuNb$_2$O$_9$ employing muon spin relaxation ($μ$SR) and $^{93}$Nb nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques. The $μ$SR zero-field relaxation rate reveals a power-law divergence of the relaxation rate as a function of temperature. Also, a power-law divergence is present in the relaxation rate as a function of applied longitudinal field, consistent with the formation of an RS phase. The $^{93}$Nb NMR spectra unambiguously resolve two components with distinct local magnetic environments, whose nature is further elucidated through spin-lattice relaxation measurements analyzed via an inverse Laplace transform (ILT) of the nuclear magnetization recovery. The relaxation-rate distribution obtained from ILT reveals two well-separated channels: a fast component, $(1/T_1)_{\mathrm{fast}}$, and a slow component, $(1/T_1)_{\mathrm{slow}}$. Both components follow distinct power-law temperature dependences ($T^α$), with $α= 0.6$ and $1.1$ for the fast and slow channels, respectively. The combined spectral and relaxation data demonstrate that the fast channel qualitatively represents an RS-like state, whereas the slow channel exhibits quantum spin liquid (QSL) like behavior, thereby establishing the microscopic coexistence of RS and QSL-like phases in Sr$_3$CuNb$_2$O$_9$.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the 3D frustrated magnet Sr₃CuNb₂O₉ using μSR and NMR techniques, discovering microscopic coexistence of random singlet (RS) and quantum spin liquid (QSL)-like phases through analysis of two distinct relaxation channels with different power-law temperature dependences.
AI.motivation: Despite recent progress identifying the exotic random singlet (RS) state in disordered frustrated magnets, 3D realizations remain scarce. Sr₃CuNb₂O₉ was proposed as a 3D frustrated system with magnetic site disorder hosting an RS ground state, requiring detailed microscopic investigation.
AI.method: Combined muon spin relaxation (μSR) and 93Nb nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques, including spin-lattice relaxation measurements analyzed via inverse Laplace transform (ILT) of nuclear magnetization recovery.
AI.result: μSR reveals power-law divergence in zero-field relaxation rate as function of temperature and applied longitudinal field, consistent with RS formation. 93Nb NMR resolves two components with distinct local magnetic environments. ILT analysis shows two well-separated relaxation channels: fast (1/T₁)_fast with α=0.6 and slow (1/T₁)_slow with α=1.1, representing RS-like and QSL-like behaviors respectively.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes microscopic coexistence of random singlet (RS) and quantum spin liquid (QSL)-like phases in Sr₃CuNb₂O₉. The fast relaxation channel corresponds to RS-like state while the slow channel exhibits QSL behavior, marking the first 3D system where both phases coexist at the microscopic level.

title: Inhibitory neuristor based on metal-to-insulator transition
summary: Mimicking the collective excitatory and inhibitory behaviors of biological neurons remains a critical challenge in the development of neuromorphic computing systems that rival the complexity and performance of the human brain. Volatile high-to-low resistance switching in insulator-to-metal transition (IMT) materials produces an abrupt increase in current flow, resembling neuronal excitation. This electrical excitation enables IMT materials to be driven into a neuron-like spiking self-oscillation regime using simple RC circuits. Here, we report a new type of self-oscillation dynamics that occurs in the opposite class of metal-to-insulator transition (MIT) materials. Electrical triggering of the MIT suppresses current flow, resembling neuronal inhibition. Using a prototypical MIT material, we experimentally demonstrate inhibitory-like self-oscillations in two-terminal switching devices incorporated into a simple RL circuit. Our results show robust ~0.1 - 1 MHz electric current oscillations with minimal cycle-to-cycle variation, which can be controlled by varying the applied DC voltage, temperature, and inductance. This work demonstrates a new type of inhibitory MIT-based artificial neuron that can complement the excitatory functionalities of IMT-based neuristors in biologically plausible neuromorphic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates a new type of inhibitory artificial neuron based on metal-to-insulator transition (MIT) materials, which complements existing excitatory insulator-to-metal transition (IMT)-based neuristors for neuromorphic computing applications.
AI.motivation: Current neuromorphic systems lack ability to mimic both excitatory and inhibitory behaviors of biological neurons; existing IMT materials only provide excitatory functionality, requiring complementary inhibitory components.
AI.method: Experimental demonstration using prototypical MIT material in two-terminal switching devices incorporated into a simple RL circuit, with control parameters including applied DC voltage, temperature, and inductance.
AI.result: Robust ~0.1-1 MHz electric current oscillations with minimal cycle-to-cycle variation were achieved, controllable through applied DC voltage, temperature, and inductance.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes MIT-based devices as inhibitory artificial neurons complementary to excitatory IMT-based neuristors, enabling biologically plausible neuromorphic computing systems with both excitation and inhibition capabilities.

title: Interaction-induced asymmetry in infinite-temperature dynamical correlations of hard-core anyons
summary: We study dynamical correlations of interacting hard-core anyons on a one-dimensional lattice at infinite temperature. This is a setting in which the many-body spectrum is independent of the statistical phase $θ$, while dynamical correlators remain sensitive to $θ$ through nonlocal Jordan-Wigner strings. We compute single-particle Green's functions, spectral functions, and density-density correlators, thereby separating the effects of fractional statistics on one-body coherence from those on density transport in a maximally mixed ensemble. In the noninteracting case $V=0$, high-temperature averaging leads to inversion-symmetric Green's functions for all $θ$ despite the presence of anyonic strings. Finite nearest-neighbor interactions $V$ generate, however, a pronounced left-right asymmetry in the Green's functions for $0<θ<π$, with the strongest chirality appearing at intermediate couplings $V\sim J$ where interactions and hopping compete most effectively. In this regime, the Green's function decays exponentially in time with a statistical-angle-dependent decay rate. At strong coupling, the dynamics crosses over to an atomic-limit regime in which the dependence on $θ$ is reduced. Here the Green's function decays universally as $t^{-1}$ and the corresponding spectral function displays a three-band structure. In contrast, density-density correlations are insensitive to statistics and recover the known infinite-temperature transport regimes of the XXZ chain, including ballistic, superdiffusive and diffusive behaviours. These results identify dynamical correlation functions as direct probes of fractional statistics in high-entropy quantum systems.

AI.tldr: This paper studies dynamical correlations of interacting hard-core anyons in 1D at infinite temperature. The authors find that while the many-body spectrum is independent of the statistical phase θ, dynamical correlators remain sensitive to θ through nonlocal Jordan-Wigner strings. They compute single-particle Green's functions, spectral functions, and density-density correlators to separate effects of fractional statistics on one-body coherence from density transport.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how fractional statistics affects dynamical properties in high-entropy quantum systems. The key insight is that spectrum independence from statistical phase θ does not imply dynamical properties are also independent - they remain sensitive through nonlocal strings.
AI.method: The authors compute single-particle Green's functions, spectral functions, and density-density correlators at infinite temperature for interacting hard-core anyons on a 1D lattice. They consider both noninteracting (V=0) and finite nearest-neighbor interaction (V) cases.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Noninteracting case shows inversion-symmetric Green's functions for all θ; (2) Finite interactions V generate left-right asymmetry for 0<θ<π, with strongest chirality at V~J; (3) At intermediate coupling: exponential decay with θ-dependent rate; (4) Strong coupling: atomic limit with reduced θ dependence, t^-1 decay, three-band spectral structure; (5) Density-density correlations are insensitive to statistics and recover XXZ chain behavior.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies dynamical correlation functions as direct probes of fractional statistics in high-entropy quantum systems. Density-density correlations are insensitive to statistics while single-particle Green's functions capture the anyonic nature through chirality and θ-dependent decay rates.

title: Symmetry-dictated switching of antiferromagnetic magnon transport in 2D multiferroics
summary: While antiferromagnetic magnons in two-dimensional (2D) materials hold immense promise for high-frequency spintronics, achieving their efficient active control remains a critical challenge. Here, we propose a universal mechanism for the nonvolatile ferroelectric (FE) switching of antiferromagnetic magnon transport in 2D multiferroic lattices. Our mechanism relies on coupling the magnon geometric phase to the FE-induced sublattice asymmetry in exchange and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions. This explicitly breaks the exact compensation of opposite-chirality magnons inherent to collinear antiferromagnets, lifting their spin degeneracy and inducing a highly tunable net Berry curvature. Crucially, reversing the FE polarization deterministically swaps these magnetic asymmetries, which completely inverts the net magnon Berry curvature and the resulting anomalous thermal Hall conductivity. Using first-principles and linear spin-wave theory, we rigorously validate this geometric-phase-driven mechanism in single-layer CuCr2Se4. Our findings establish a robust paradigm for coupling multiferroicity with the magnon geometric phase, paving the way for nonvolatile and electrically switchable antiferromagnetic magnonics.

AI.tldr: Proposes a mechanism for nonvolatile ferroelectric switching of antiferromagnetic magnon transport in 2D multiferroic materials by coupling magnon geometric phase to FE-induced sublattice asymmetry, validated in single-layer CuCr2Se4.
AI.motivation: Antiferromagnetic magnons in 2D materials are promising for high-frequency spintronics, but achieving efficient active control remains a critical challenge.
AI.method: The mechanism relies on coupling the magnon geometric phase to FE-induced sublattice asymmetry in exchange and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions. Validated using first-principles calculations and linear spin-wave theory in single-layer CuCr2Se4.
AI.result: The mechanism breaks the spin degeneracy of opposite-chirality magnons, inducing tunable net Berry curvature. Reversing FE polarization completely inverts the net magnon Berry curvature and resulting anomalous thermal Hall conductivity.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a robust paradigm for coupling multiferroicity with the magnon geometric phase, enabling nonvolatile and electrically switchable antiferromagnetic magnonics.

title: Gauge-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Lattice Gauge Theories
summary: Local gauge symmetry underlies fundamental interactions and strongly correlated quantum matter, yet existing machine-learning approaches lack a general, principled framework for learning under site-dependent symmetries, particularly for intrinsically nonlocal observables. Here we introduce a gauge-equivariant graph neural network that embeds non-Abelian symmetry directly into message passing via matrix-valued, gauge-covariant features and symmetry-compatible updates, extending equivariant learning from global to fully local symmetries. In this formulation, message passing implements gauge-covariant transport across the lattice, allowing nonlocal correlations and loop-like structures to emerge naturally from local operations. We validate the approach across pure gauge, gauge-matter, and dynamical regimes, establishing gauge-equivariant message passing as a general paradigm for learning in systems governed by local symmetry.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces gauge-equivariant graph neural networks that embed non-Abelian symmetry into message passing via matrix-valued, gauge-covariant features, enabling learning of nonlocal observables in gauge theories.
AI.motivation: Existing machine-learning approaches lack a general framework for learning under site-dependent (local) symmetries, particularly for intrinsically nonlocal observables like loop-like structures in gauge theories.
AI.method: Gauge-equivariant graph neural network embedding non-Abelian symmetry directly into message passing via matrix-valued, gauge-covariant features and symmetry-compatible updates. Message passing implements gauge-covariant transport across lattice, allowing nonlocal correlations and loop structures to emerge from local operations.
AI.result: Validated across pure gauge, gauge-matter, and dynamical regimes, establishing gauge-equivariant message passing as a general paradigm for learning in systems governed by local (gauge) symmetry.
AI.conclusion: This framework extends equivariant learning from global to fully local symmetries, providing a principled approach for learning in strongly correlated quantum matter and fundamental interactions governed by gauge symmetry.

title: Critical Activation Voltage for Phonon-Mediated Field-Driven Phenomena
summary: Field-driven phenomena, from flash sintering to electromigration, exhibit threshold fields spanning six orders of magnitude. We show their product with the onset activation coherence length is a universal critical activation voltage, Vc =0.1-2.7 V. Vc represents the threshold electrical work required to resonantly couple to the universal phonon damping peak where lattice softening is maximized. This invariant unifies macroscopic thermal instabilities with the nanoscale Blech limit, establishing a universal phenomenological law for field-lattice coupling across 17 crystal families

AI.tldr: A universal critical activation voltage (Vc = 0.1-2.7 V) was discovered that represents the threshold electrical work required to resonantly couple to the phonon damping peak where lattice softening is maximized. This invariant unifies macroscopic thermal instabilities with the nanoscale Blech limit, establishing a universal phenomenological law for field-lattice coupling across 17 crystal families.
AI.motivation: To find a universal law that explains field-driven phenomena (like flash sintering and electromigration) which exhibit threshold fields spanning six orders of magnitude, and to unify macroscopic thermal instabilities with the nanoscale Blech limit.
AI.method: The researchers identified a universal critical activation voltage (Vc) by examining the product of threshold fields with the onset activation coherence length. They found this voltage corresponds to the threshold electrical work needed to resonantly couple to the universal phonon damping peak where lattice softening is maximized.
AI.result: The product of threshold fields with onset activation coherence length yields a universal critical activation voltage Vc = 0.1-2.7 V. This invariant was found to be consistent across 17 crystal families.
AI.conclusion: The discovery of this universal critical activation voltage (Vc) establishes a universal phenomenological law for field-lattice coupling across diverse materials, unifying macroscopic thermal instabilities with the nanoscale Blech limit.

title: Unconventional Quantum Criticality in Long-Range Spin-1 Chains: Insights from Entanglement Entropy and Bipartite Fluctuations
summary: We study the ground-state phase diagram of a spin-1 Heisenberg chain with staggered long-range (LR) interactions decaying as $\propto r^{-α}$ using a quantum Monte Carlo approach based on the split-spin representation. This formulation enables efficient large-scale simulations by mapping the spin-1 model onto spin-$1/2$ degrees of freedom with local projection constraints. We resolve the continuous quantum phase transition between the gapped Haldane phase at large $α$ (short-range regime) and a gapless antiferromagnetically ordered Néel phase at small $α$ (LR regime), where the continuous SU(2) symmetry is broken. From finite-size scaling and crossing point analyses, we determine the critical point to be at $α_c = 2.48(2)$ and extract the associated critical exponents, which indicate unconventional criticality. In particular, the transition is found to be nonconformal, characterized by a dynamic exponent $z \neq 1$. We further analyze the scaling of entanglement entropy and bipartite fluctuations across the transition, and determine the corresponding universal scalings in both phases and at criticality.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the ground-state phase diagram of a spin-1 Heisenberg chain with long-rangeInteractions decaying as r^(-α) using quantum Monte Carlo. It identifies a continuous quantum phase transition at α_c = 2.48(2) between the gapped Haldane phase and gapless Néel phase, with unconventional nonconformal criticality characterized by dynamic exponent z ≠ 1.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the quantum phase transition in a spin-1 Heisenberg chain with long-range interactions decaying as r^(-α). The motivation stems from understanding how the competition between short-range Heisenberg exchange and long-range interactions affects the ground-state phase diagram, particularly the transition between the gapped Haldane phase and gapless antiferromagnetic Néel phase.
AI.method: The authors employ quantum Monte Carlo simulations using a split-spin representation that maps spin-1 degrees of freedom onto spin-1/2 variables with local projection constraints. This approach enables efficient large-scale simulations. Finite-size scaling and crossing point analyses are used to determine the critical exponents, along with analysis of entanglement entropy and bipartite fluctuations.
AI.result: The critical point is determined to be at α_c = 2.48(2), marking the continuous quantum phase transition between the Haldane phase at large α and the Néel phase at small α. The transition exhibits unconventional criticality characterized by a dynamic exponent z ≠ 1, indicating nonconformal behavior. Universal scalings for entanglement entropy and bipartite fluctuations are extracted in both phases and at criticality.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully identifies a continuous quantum phase transition in spin-1 Heisenberg chains with long-range interactions, with the critical point at α_c ≈ 2.48. The transition is found to be nonconformal with z ≠ 1, representing an example of unconventional criticality beyond standard conformal field theory predictions. The work provides comprehensive scaling analysis across the phase diagram.

title: Resonance-enhanced super-superexchange yields giant chiral magnon splitting in rutile altermagnets
summary: Altermagnets host momentum-selective spin splitting and chiral-split magnonic excitations despite vanishing net magnetization, enabling spin transport without ferromagnetism. In rutile structures, establishing altermagnetism spectroscopically has been challenging, motivating the search for a rutile platform with a resolvable exchange-driven chiral magnon splitting. Here we combine hybrid-functional first-principles calculations with linear spin-wave theory to show that rutile CuF$_2$ exhibits a meV-scale splitting between magnon modes of opposite chirality along momentum directions dictated by its $d$-wave altermagnetic symmetry. The splitting originates from an anomalously strong long-range super-superexchange channel Cu--F$\cdots$F--Cu, which enhances the symmetry-allowed difference between seventh-neighbour exchanges, $J_{7b} - J_{7a}$, controlling the chiral-mode splitting. We identify an orbital-resonance mechanism: energetic alignment between Cu $3d_{z^2}$ and F $2p_z$ states strengthens virtual hopping along the Cu--F$\cdots$F--Cu path and amplifies the anisotropic long-range exchange. Rutile CuF$_2$ therefore provides an ideal platform to validate rutile altermagnetism and suggests an orbital-energy description for engineering large chiral magnon splittings in insulating altermagnets.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that rutile CuF2 exhibits meV-scale chiral magnon splitting due to strong long-range super-superexchange interactions, providing an ideal platform to validate rutile altermagnetism through first-principles calculations combined with linear spin-wave theory.
AI.motivation: Rutile structures have been challenging for establishing altermagnetism spectroscopically, motivating the search for a rutile platform with resolvable exchange-driven chiral magnon splitting.
AI.method: Hybrid-functional first-principles calculations combined with linear spin-wave theory were used to investigate CuF2.
AI.result: Rutile CuF2 exhibits meV-scale splitting between magnon modes of opposite chirality along momentum directions dictated by its d-wave altermagnetic symmetry. The splitting originates from anomalously strong long-range super-superexchange channel Cu--F···F--Cu, which enhances the symmetry-allowed difference between seventh-neighbor exchanges (J7b - J7a).
AI.conclusion: Rutile CuF2 provides an ideal platform to validate rutile altermagnetism and suggests an orbital-energy description for engineering large chiral magnon splittings in insulating altermagnets.

title: Layer-mediated tuning of spin and valley physics in stacked tetragonal altermagnetic bilayers
summary: As an emerging magnetic phase, altermagnets (AMs) with collinear compensated magnetism in real space and alternating spin splitting in the band structure have attracted widespread attention. Here, based on first-principles calculations, we demonstrate that the layer stacking imposes symmetry constraints on the spin and valley degrees of freedom (DOFs) in an AM bilayer composed of two tetragonal altermagnetic monolayers, thereby enabling the tuning of these DOFs through interlayer sliding as well as by an external electric field. Using several representative AM bilayers, we reveal that the [C2||P] and [C2||Mz] symmetries intrinsically enforce spin degeneracy, while the coupling between spin and layer DOFs establishes a general framework for achieving electric field control of spin states. Appropriate interlayer sliding breaks the [C2||Md] symmetry of AM bilayers, thereby giving rise to a spontaneous valley splitting and driving a transition to a fully compensated ferrimagnetic state. Furthermore, owing to the tunable valley splitting induced by interlayer sliding, enhanced tunneling magnetoresistance (TMR) can be realized by AM bilayers. This work highlights the intrinsic correlation among spin, valley, and layer DOFs, offering symmetry-based design principles for layer-based spintronic and valleytronic devices.

AI.tldr: This work demonstrates that layer stacking symmetry constraints in altermagnetic bilayers enable tuning of spin and valley degrees of freedom through interlayer sliding and external electric fields, with potential applications in spintronic and valleytronic devices.
AI.motivation: Altermagnets are an emerging magnetic phase with unique properties (collinear compensated magnetism in real space and alternating spin splitting in band structure), but the effect of layer stacking on their spin and valley degrees of freedom was not well understood.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were used to study several representative altermagnetic bilayers, examining symmetry constraints imposed by layer stacking on spin and valley degrees of freedom.
AI.result: The [C2||P] and [C2||Mz] symmetries enforce spin degeneracy in AM bilayers; interlayer sliding breaks [C2||Md] symmetry, causing spontaneous valley splitting and driving a transition to a fully compensated ferrimagnetic state; tunable valley splitting enables enhanced tunneling magnetoresistance (TMR).
AI.conclusion: This work provides symmetry-based design principles for layer-based spintronic and valleytronic devices, highlighting the intrinsic correlation among spin, valley, and layer degrees of freedom.

title: Crystal structure prediction with nuclear quantum and finite-temperature effects via deep free energy learning
summary: Accurate crystal structure prediction (CSP) requires accounting for finite-temperature and nuclear quantum effects, yet first-principles evaluation of the free energy surface (FES) remains prohibitive for high-throughput searches. We observe that the self-consistent harmonic approximation (SCHA) FES, as a function of nuclear centroid positions, shares the same mathematical structure as a potential-energy surface and can therefore be directly learned by a deep neural network potential. The resulting deep free energy (DF) model, constructed via a two-level concurrent-learning workflow, evaluates free energies, forces, and stresses in a single forward pass. Applied to the La-Sc-H system at 200 GPa and 300 K, DF-based CSP reproduces the stability of the experimentally observed LaH10 and LaSc2H24, and discovers an unreported thermodynamically stable clathrate hydride: P4/mmm LaScH8. Benchmarked on the LaH10 system, the DF model achieves a 1.72*10^6-fold cost reduction relative to DFT-level SSCHA. The DF framework provides a scalable route for incorporating finite-temperature and nuclear quantum effects into high-throughput crystal structure prediction.

AI.tldr: A deep learning approach (DF model) combines SCHA with neural network potentials to enable computationally efficient free energy surface predictions for crystal structure prediction, achieving 1.72 million-fold speedup over DFT-level methods and discovering a new stable hydride compound.
AI.motivation: Accurate crystal structure prediction (CSP) requires accounting for finite-temperature and nuclear quantum effects, but first-principles evaluation of the free energy surface (FES) remains prohibitive for high-throughput searches.
AI.method: The SCHA FES (as a function of nuclear centroid positions) shares the same mathematical structure as a potential-energy surface and can be directly learned by a deep neural network potential. A two-level concurrent-learning workflow constructs the deep free energy (DF) model, which evaluates free energies, forces, and stresses in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Applied to La-Sc-H system at 200 GPa and 300 K, DF-based CSP reproduces stability of experimentally observed LaH10 and LaSc2H24, and discovers an unreported thermodynamically stable clathrate hydride: P4/mmm LaScH8. The DF model achieves 1.72×10^6-fold cost reduction relative to DFT-level SSCHA.
AI.conclusion: The DF framework provides a scalable route for incorporating finite-temperature and nuclear quantum effects into high-throughput crystal structure prediction.

title: Domain-Wall-Mediated Ultralow-Barrier Sliding and Pinning in Ferroelectric Moiré Superlattices Revealed by Machine Learning
summary: Sliding ferroelectrics built from stacked nonpolar monolayers enable out-of-plane polarization and unconventional switching via interlayer sliding, yet the microscopic sliding dynamics remain unclear. Using machine-learning molecular dynamics, we reveal spontaneous thermally driven interlayer sliding in ferroelectric MoS2 moiré superlattices, with relative velocities on the order of 1 m/s at 300 K. Instead of rigid translation of the entire bilayer, the motion appears as a global drift of the moiré pattern. Such thermally driven sliding is inconsistent with the meV/atom-scale rigid-sliding barrier. In contrast, when constrained relaxation is allowed, the sliding proceeds along an almost barrierless pathway that directly reproduces the global drift of the moiré pattern. Furthermore, sulfur vacancies trigger a sliding-to-pinning transition, with about 0.1% S vacancies already sufficient to convert the long-range sliding into localized oscillations. Notably, these phenomena are not restricted to small twist angles, but arise generically in twisting-induced multidomain structures. These results reveal that the sliding process is governed by a domain-wall-mediated collective reconstruction pathway with an ultralow barrier, rather than rigid layer translation, deepening the understanding of microscopic dynamics in moiré superlattices and sliding ferroelectrics.

AI.tldr: This paper uses machine-learning molecular dynamics to investigate microscopic sliding dynamics in ferroelectric MoS2 moiré superlattices. The study reveals that interlayer sliding occurs as a global drift of the moiré pattern with velocities around 1 m/s at room temperature, rather than rigid layer translation. Sulfur vacancies convert long-range sliding into localized oscillations. The sliding process is governed by a domain-wall-mediated collective reconstruction pathway with ultralow barrier.
AI.motivation: Sliding ferroelectrics built from stacked nonpolar monolayers enable out-of-plane polarization and unconventional switching via interlayer sliding, but the microscopic sliding dynamics remained unclear. The researchers aimed to understand the mechanism of thermally driven interlayer sliding at the atomic level.
AI.method: Machine-learning molecular dynamics simulations were used to study ferroelectric MoS2 moiré superlattices. The simulations examined spontaneous thermally driven interlayer sliding, compared rigid translation vs. barrierless pathways under constrained relaxation, and investigated the effect of sulfur vacancies on sliding behavior.
AI.result: The study found: (1) Spontaneous thermally driven interlayer sliding with velocities of ~1 m/s at 300 K, appearing as global drift of moiré pattern rather than rigid translation; (2) Thermally driven sliding is inconsistent with meV/atom-scale rigid-sliding barrier; (3) Constrained relaxation allows barrierless pathway reproducing global drift; (4) ~0.1% S vacancies convert long-range sliding into localized oscillations (sliding-to-pinning transition); (5) These phenomena arise generically in twisting-induced multidomain structures, not restricted to small twist angles.
AI.conclusion: The sliding process in moiré superlattices and sliding ferroelectrics is governed by a domain-wall-mediated collective reconstruction pathway with ultralow barrier, rather than rigid layer translation. This deepens understanding of microscopic dynamics in these materials and provides insights for designing sliding ferroelectric devices.

title: LLM-guided phase diagram construction through high-throughput experimentation
summary: Constructing phase diagrams for multicomponent alloys requires extensive experimental measurements and is a time-consuming task. Here we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can guide experimental planning for phase diagram construction. In our framework, a general-purpose LLM serves as the experimental planner, suggesting compositions for measurement at each cycle in a closed loop with high-throughput synthesis and X-ray diffraction phase identification. Using this framework, we experimentally constructed the ternary phase diagram of the Co-Al-Ge system at 900 degree C through iterative synthesis and characterization. We compared two strategies that differ in how the initial compositions are selected: one uses predictions from a domain-specific LLM trained on phase diagram data (aLLoyM), while the other relies solely on the general-purpose LLM. The two strategies exhibited complementary strengths. aLLoyM directed the initial measurements toward compositionally complex regions in the interior of the ternary diagram, enabling the earliest discovery of all three novel phases that form only in the ternary system. In contrast, the general-purpose LLM adopted a textbook-like approach which efficiently identified a larger number of phases in fewer cycles. In addition, a simulated benchmark comparing the LLM against conventional machine learning confirmed that the LLM achieves more efficient exploration. The results demonstrate that LLMs have high potential as experimental planners for phase diagram construction.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that LLMs can serve as effective experimental planners for constructing ternary phase diagrams. A domain-specific LLM (aLLoyM) was better at finding complex ternary phases early, while a general-purpose LLM was more efficient at identifying more phases with fewer measurements. The LLM approach outperformed conventional machine learning in simulations.
AI.motivation: Traditional phase diagram construction requires extensive experimental measurements, which is time-consuming and resource-intensive. The authors investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can serve as experimental planners to guide the selection of compositions for measurement in a closed-loop with high-throughput synthesis and X-ray diffraction.
AI.method: The authors developed a framework where a general-purpose LLM suggests compositions for measurement in each cycle, coupled with high-throughput synthesis and XRD phase identification. They compared two strategies: (1) using predictions from a domain-specific LLM (aLLoyM) trained on phase diagram data, and (2) relying solely on the general-purpose LLM. They experimentally constructed the ternary phase diagram of the Co-Al-Ge system at 900°C through iterative synthesis and characterization.
AI.result: The two strategies showed complementary strengths. aLLoyM directed initial measurements toward compositionally complex regions in the interior of the ternary diagram, enabling the earliest discovery of all three novel ternary phases. In contrast, the general-purpose LLM adopted a textbook-like approach that efficiently identified a larger number of phases in fewer cycles. Additionally, a simulated benchmark showed that the LLM achieves more efficient exploration compared to conventional machine learning.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have high potential as experimental planners for phase diagram construction, demonstrating complementary strengths between domain-specific and general-purpose models in guiding materials discovery.

title: Multilayer Laue Lenses for Enhanced Spatial Resolution in Dark-Field X-ray Microscopy
summary: We introduce the use of a crossed pair of Multilayer Laue Lenses (MLLs) as an objective in Dark-Field X-ray Microscopy (DFXM). In a demonstration experiment at the ID03 beamline at ESRF, two flat Mo-Si MLLs were used, with a physical aperture of 50 x 50 $\mathrm{μm^2}$ and a focal length of 14.25 mm at 19 keV. Applying a 10 % criterion to the Modulation Transfer Functions (MTFs) acquired, a spatial resolution of 56 nm is obtained in bright-field mode -- more than three times better than with a compound refractive lens (CRL) objective. The dark-field resolution is similar. With an efficiency of 26.7 % the MLL objective expands the science domain of DFXM significantly, both for bulk and near-surface studies. Similar to the CRL case, the reciprocal space resolution is dominated by the numerical aperture (NA) of the objective, with the NA being three times larger in the MLL case. This enables faster orientation mapping and implies improved options for the use of tomographic reconstruction algorithms. Although the MLL objective pupil varies with energy and position, secondary peaks are suppressed, simplifying both interpretation and forward simulations. We present an example DFXM application using the MLL as an objective, imaging a through-silicon via Kelvin device.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates the use of Multilayer Laue Lenses (MLLs) as objectives in Dark-Field X-ray Microscopy (DFXM), achieving 56 nm spatial resolution at 19 keV - more than three times better than compound refractive lens (CRL) objectives, with 26.7% efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve spatial resolution and efficiency in DFXM for bulk and near-surface studies. Previous CRL-based DFXM had limited resolution, and the authors seek to expand the science domain of DFXM through better optical elements.
AI.method: The authors used two flat Mo-Si MLLs in a crossed pair configuration as the objective, with physical aperture of 50×50 μm² and focal length of 14.25 mm at 19 keV. Experiments were conducted at the ID03 beamline at ESRF. Resolution was assessed using Modulation Transfer Functions (MTFs) with a 10% criterion.
AI.result: The MLL objective achieved 56 nm spatial resolution in bright-field mode (more than 3× better than CRL), with similar dark-field resolution. Efficiency reached 26.7%. The numerical aperture (NA) was 3× larger than CRL, enabling faster orientation mapping and improved tomographic reconstruction. Secondary peaks were suppressed despite varying pupil with energy and position.
AI.conclusion: The MLL objective significantly expands the science domain of DFXM for both bulk and near-surface studies. The 3× larger NA enables faster orientation mapping and better tomographic reconstruction. The suppressed secondary peaks simplify interpretation and forward simulations. An application demonstration imaging a through-silicon via Kelvin device is presented.

title: Fluctuation-driven multi-step charge density wave transition in monolayer TiSe$_2$
summary: The exact microscopic origin, symmetry, and thermal melting mechanism of the charge density wave (CDW) phase in TiSe$_{2}$ remain a subject of intense debate, particularly regarding the presence of chiral structural order and a multi-step phase transition. Here, we resolve the finite-temperature structural dynamics of the monolayer TiSe$_{2}$ using large-scale molecular dynamics simulations driven by an accurate, first-principles-trained machine-learning interatomic potential. We demonstrate that the CDW melting deviates from a conventional second-order phase transition, while it undergoes a two-step melting process characterised by an extended fluctuation regime between $T^{\ast}\approx200$ K and $T_{\mathrm{CDW}}\approx250$ K, with proliferation of topological defects and domain walls, and accompanied by a completely overdamped soft optical phonon. Furthermore, we reveal that anisotropic long-wavelength thermal fluctuations spontaneously stabilise an asymmetric $3Q$ chiral CDW order with $C2$ symmetry. These findings provide a unified microscopic framework for understanding complex fluctuation-driven phase transitions in 2D quantum materials, demonstrating that the intricate CDW physics of TiSe$_{2}$ can be largely captured without invoking excitonic correlations.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the finite-temperature structural dynamics of monolayer TiSe2 using machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics, revealing a two-step CDW melting process with an extended fluctuation regime and the emergence of chiral CDW order driven by anisotropic thermal fluctuations, without requiring excitonic correlations.
AI.motivation: The microscopic origin, symmetry, and thermal melting mechanism of the charge density wave (CDW) phase in TiSe2 remain intensely debated, particularly regarding the presence of chiral structural order and multi-step phase transition. This study aims to resolve the finite-temperature structural dynamics of monolayer TiSe2.
AI.method: Large-scale molecular dynamics simulations driven by an accurate, first-principles-trained machine-learning interatomic potential were employed to investigate the CDW phase transition.
AI.result: The study reveals that CDW melting deviates from a conventional second-order phase transition, exhibiting a two-step melting process with an extended fluctuation regime between T*≈200 K and TCDW≈250 K. This involves proliferation of topological defects and domain walls, accompanied by a completely overdamped soft optical phonon. Additionally, anisotropic long-wavelength thermal fluctuations spontaneously stabilize an asymmetric 3Q chiral CDW order with C2 symmetry.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide a unified microscopic framework for understanding complex fluctuation-driven phase transitions in 2D quantum materials, demonstrating that the intricate CDW physics of TiSe2 can be largely captured without invoking excitonic correlations.

title: Second-order topology in two-dimensional azulenoid kekulene carbon lattices
summary: The discovery of higher-order topological insulator (HOTI) has established a new paradigm for understanding symmetry-constrained boundary electronic states. Here, based on first-principles calculations, we demonstrate the emergence of HOTI phase in organic lattices of two-dimensional azulenoid-kekulene-type carbon allotropes, namely AKC-[3,3] and AKC-[6,0]. Enabled by the $C_6$ rotational symmetry, the nontrivial bulk topology is confirmed through the topological invariant and fractionally quantized corner charge, giving $\{[M^{(I)}_{2}],[K^{(3)}_{2}]\}$ = $\{0,2\}$ and $Q_{\mathrm{corner}} = e/3$, respectively, accompanied by the emergence of exotic corner states in nanoflakes. Notably, the structural modifications are explored, revealing that in the derived structure PAK-[6,0], whose corner-localized states are preserved, highlighting the robustness of the higher-order topological phase. These findings highlight azulenoid-kekulene-based carbon allotropes as a promising platform to explore the interplay between structural design, crystalline symmetry, and higher-order topological boundary responses in two dimensional carbon systems.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates higher-order topological insulator (HOTI) phases in organic azulenoid-kekulene carbon allotropes (AKC-[3,3] and AKC-[6,0]) using first-principles calculations, showing fractionally quantized corner charge (e/3) and exotic corner states in nanoflakes, with robust topological phases even in modified structures.
AI.motivation: Higher-order topological insulators represent a new paradigm for understanding symmetry-constrained boundary electronic states. The authors seek to discover HOTI phases in 2D carbon systems, specifically in organic lattices of azulenoid-kekulene-type carbon allotropes, which could provide a promising platform for exploring the interplay between structural design, crystalline symmetry, and higher-order topological boundary responses.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were performed to investigate the electronic structure and topological properties of two-dimensional azulenoid-kekulene carbon allotropes (AKC-[3,3] and AKC-[6,0]). The topological invariant was calculated, and fractionally quantized corner charge was examined. The C6 rotational symmetry was leveraged to confirm nontrivial bulk topology. Structural modifications were explored to test the robustness of the higher-order topological phase.
AI.result: The nontrivial bulk topology was confirmed through the topological invariant {-[M^{(I)}_{2}],[K^{(3)}_{2}]} = {0,2} and fractionally quantized corner charge Q_corner = e/3. Exotic corner states emerged in nanoflakes. The derived structure PAK-[6,0] preserved corner-localized states, demonstrating the robustness of the higher-order topological phase.
AI.conclusion: Azulenoid-kekulene-based carbon allotropes represent a promising platform to explore the interplay between structural design, crystalline symmetry, and higher-order topological boundary responses in 2D carbon systems. The findings establish HOTI phases in organic carbon lattices and highlight the robustness of topological phases under structural modifications.

title: Phonon driven non-equilibrium triggers for thermal runaway in battery electrodes
summary: Thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries is governed by the poorly-understood initiation phase, where localised heating introduces instability. Here we identify the three key components that trigger thermal runaway, decreases in local conductivity, heat capacity changes, and intercalation heating, which significantly increase temperature gradients that accelerate battery degradation. Using a multiscale framework that links atomistic phonon calculations with grain-resolved thermal modelling, we identify large thermal gradients across grain boundaries arising from external heating events and intercalation-dependent thermal properties of Li$_x$ZrS$_2$. The observed changes in thermal conductivity are due to charge redistribution and bond-strength modulation of the host, in contrast to the existing theory of lithium rattler mechanics. Internal heating events driven by intercalation gives rise to local thermal gradients, finite-speed thermal wave interference, and internal thermal fluctuations that generate mechanical strain and sub-grain thermal breakdown. These results show that the trigger for thermal runaway is controlled by internal grain architecture and composition, as well as the external environment. Our findings establish materials and electrode design rules for suppressing hotspot formation and improving battery safety during fast charging.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies three key triggers of thermal runaway in Li-ion batteries (decreased local conductivity, heat capacity changes, and intercalation heating) using a multiscale phonon-thermal modeling framework. It reveals that thermal gradients across grain boundaries, caused by charge redistribution and bond-strength modulation, lead to mechanical strain and thermal breakdown, providing design rules for safer batteries during fast charging.
AI.motivation: Thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries is governed by the poorly-understood initiation phase, where localised heating introduces instability. This study aimed to identify the key components that trigger thermal runaway.
AI.method: A multiscale framework that links atomistic phonon calculations with grain-resolved thermal modelling was used to study Li_xZrS_2 across grain boundaries.
AI.result: Three key components trigger thermal runaway: decreases in local conductivity, heat capacity changes, and intercalation heating. These significantly increase temperature gradients that accelerate battery degradation. Large thermal gradients across grain boundaries arise from external heating events and intercalation-dependent thermal properties. Changes in thermal conductivity are due to charge redistribution and bond-strength modulation of the host, in contrast to the existing theory of lithium rattler mechanics. Internal heating events driven by intercalation gives rise to local thermal gradients, finite-speed thermal wave interference, and internal thermal fluctuations that generate mechanical strain and sub-grain thermal breakdown.
AI.conclusion: The trigger for thermal runaway is controlled by internal grain architecture and composition, as well as the external environment. These findings establish materials and electrode design rules for suppressing hotspot formation and improving battery safety during fast charging.

title: Including nanoparticle shape into macrospin models
